title: Moroccan lentil soup
subtitle: A spiced broth-based soup of lentils, chickpeas, and tomato.
excerpt: The lighter cousin of harira. Red lentils, chickpeas, and a tomato-spiced broth, simmered for thirty-five minutes, finished with chopped coriander and a squeeze of lemon. A weeknight dinner-and-leftovers soup that improves overnight.
sourceNotes: Moroccan lentil soup is the everyday weeknight sibling of harira, the richer Ramadan soup. The two share spice profile (cumin, coriander, turmeric, ginger), tomato, and lentils, but the everyday version drops harira's meat, vermicelli, and yeast-raised thickener. The dish belongs to a broad North African daily-soup tradition shared with Algerian chorba and Tunisian shorba. The lemon and chopped coriander at the end is the standard Moroccan brightening; the bowl tastes flat without them.

content.text: Red lentils dissolve almost completely into the broth, which thickens the soup without any cornflour. Brown lentils hold their shape and the soup is brothier. The recipe below uses red, which is faster and softer; if you prefer texture, swap half for brown and add ten minutes to the simmer time.

The spices are toasted in oil with the onion, not added with the liquid. Toasting wakes up the cumin and coriander; raw they taste dusty in the bowl. A half-blend with a stick blender at the end gives a softer texture without losing the chunks of carrot and celery. The lemon at the table is not optional; the soup tastes flat without it.

Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} chopped onion, the {{celery}} of chopped celery, the {{carrot}} of diced carrot, and a pinch of salt. Cook for ten minutes, stirring every couple of minutes, until softened and starting to colour at the edges.

Add the {{garlic}} of chopped garlic and the {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger; cook for one minute. Stir in the {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, the {{coriander-ground}} of coriander, the {{turmeric}} of turmeric, the {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, and the {{cinnamon-ground}} of cinnamon. Cook for one minute, stirring constantly; the spices should smell of themselves rather than of raw powder. Add the {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and cook for one minute more, until darkened.

Tip in the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of crushed tomatoes, the {{red-lentils}} of rinsed red lentils, the {{chickpeas-tinned}} of drained chickpeas, and the {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Bring to a boil, then lower to a steady simmer. Cook for thirty minutes, stirring every five or so minutes to stop the lentils sticking. The lentils dissolve into the soup; the carrots and chickpeas hold their shape.

Pull off the heat. If you like a smoother soup, give it three or four pulses with a stick blender; do not blend smooth, the texture should still carry chunks of vegetable. Taste; add salt and a turn of pepper. Stir through half the {{coriander}} of chopped coriander and half the {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley. Ladle into bowls and finish each with the rest of the herbs and a wedge of {{lemon}} to squeeze over.

items.fix: Keep the heat at a gentle simmer; lentils sink and stick at a hard boil. Stir every five minutes with a wooden spoon scraping the base.
items.cause: Simmer was too high, or stirring not frequent enough.
items.symptom: Lentils stuck and burnt at the base of the pan

items.fix: Loosen with hot stock or boiling water; the lentils continue to absorb after the heat is off.
items.cause: Too much lentil for the liquid, or simmered too long.

items.fix: Always cook the spice blend in oil with the aromatics for at least one minute before any liquid hits the pan. Raw spice in soup never recovers.
items.cause: Not toasted in the oil before the liquid went in.

items.fix: Salt while cooking, not just at the end. The lemon at the table is the brightening agent; do not skip it.
items.cause: Under-salted, or the lemon was forgotten.

items.fix: Rinse red lentils in a fine sieve under cold water until the water runs clear. The lentils carry a fine dust that turns to grit in the pot.

content.text: Half brown lentils and half red gives a soup with more texture; cook an extra ten minutes. Add a handful of cooked broken vermicelli at the end for a fuller bowl. A spoon of harissa stirred in at the table for those who want heat. For a thicker, more substantial version closer to harira, add 200 g of cubed lamb shoulder browned with the onion and cook for an additional twenty minutes.

Daily soup is universal across North African home cooking, and lentil-and-chickpea versions are the year-round backbone. The Moroccan version leans on warm spices (cinnamon, ginger, cumin) and finishes with herbs and lemon. The Tunisian shorba runs hotter on chilli; the Algerian chorba adds more vermicelli and a richer tomato base. All three turn cheap ingredients into a complete meal with bread on the side.
